habitat: Shaded hill streams, evergreen and semi-evergreen forests, moist valleys, forest edges near water and ravines. Strongly tied to flowing water across the Western Ghats.

description.adult_male: Large, glossy dark blue-black thrush with intense electric-blue patches on shoulders, scapulars and sometimes forehead. Plumage shines differently under varying light, shifting from deep blackish-blue to metallic cobalt. Bill strong and dark; legs dark grey. Eyes deep-set, giving a bold expression.
description.adult_female: Similar to male but generally duller; blue patches less extensive and less intensely metallic. Throat and breast may appear slightly browner or sootier. Structure and proportions identical.
description.juvenile: Duller sooty-brown overall with faint mottling on breast and flanks. Blue patches largely absent or muted. Wings and scapulars may show slight bluish sheen as the bird matures. Bill and legs paler than adults.
description.in_flight: Strong, direct flight low over streams or through forest corridors. Blue shoulder patches flash as wings open. Tail slightly rounded; flight usually between favoured perches along watercourses.

behaviour: Often seen near forest streams, hopping from boulders to roots and inspecting wet leaf litter for prey. Extremely vocal at dawn and dusk; famous for its rich, fluty, human-like whistled phrases that carry far through forest valleys. Also produces sharp metallic 'tink' notes. Will sit motionless on shaded perches for long periods before dropping to forage. Territorial males sing from exposed rocks or low branches near water. Nests typically constructed in crevices of banks, behind waterfalls or on rock ledges above streams.
food: Insects, larvae, snails, crabs, small amphibians and other streamside prey. Regularly flips leaves and probes crevices for invertebrates; occasionally takes small fish or tadpoles.

photos.caption: Adult near a shaded stream at Mangaon, Raigad.

habitat: Dense undergrowth in moist evergreen and semi-evergreen forests, bamboo thickets, shaded plantations, and well-vegetated gullies. Prefers dense, tangled understory with low perches and concealed pathways.

description.adult_male: Striking shama with glossy black head, mantle and upperparts; bright chestnut belly and flanks; very long black tail with white outer feathers and a conspicuous white rump patch that flares in flight. Eyes dark and expressive. Posture upright and alert, often flicking tail.
description.adult_female: Duller and browner than the male. Upperparts grey-brown to olive-brown; belly pale rufous to buff; tail shorter and less dramatic. White rump less boldly contrasted. Lacks the male's deep glossy black sheen.
description.juvenile: Heavily scaled or mottled above and below with rich brown and buff spotting. Tail short; white rump indistinct. Gradually transitions into adult plumage over several months, with young males beginning to show darker head and longer tail feathers first.
description.in_flight: Fluid and agile flight through dense undergrowth. White rump and outer tail feathers flash distinctly when the tail spreads during short flights or while landing. Frequently flicks long tail downward as it lands on low perches.

behaviour: Elusive and shy, usually staying low inside tangles or bamboo clumps. Despite being hard to see, it is among the finest songsters of the region: rich, melodious, fluty phrases delivered from a hidden perch, often at dawn. Males also mimic other birds. Forages on or near the ground, hopping quietly between shadows for insects. Territorial males sing year-round but intensify during breeding season. When alarmed, adopts an erect posture and flicks its long tail sharply. Nests in tree cavities, bamboo hollows or crevices, lined with roots and leaves.
food: Primarily insects—beetles, termites, ants, caterpillars, grasshoppers and small invertebrates. Occasionally takes small berries and fruits.

photos.caption: Adult male showing long tail and bold white rump.
Female white-rumped shama perched in foliage.

habitat: Gardens, orchards, coffee and tea plantations, village edges, forest edges and clearings, scrub, and urban parks. Closely associated with shrubs and small trees, especially where fruiting plants and hedges are present.

description.adult_male: Slender brown bulbul with a distinctive pointed black crest and prominent white cheeks. A bright red 'whisker' patch behind the eye contrasts with the white cheek and dark head. Upperparts warm brown; underparts whitish with a darker brown breast. Vent and undertail coverts bright red. Tail fairly long and dark with white tips to the outer feathers. Bill and legs dark; eye dark.
description.adult_female: Plumage essentially identical to male; sexes alike in the field with the same black crest, red whisker patch, red vent and white-tipped tail. Slightly smaller on average but not reliably distinguishable.
description.juvenile: Crest shorter and browner; red whisker patch and vent duller or partially developed. Underparts buffier, with less contrast between breast and belly. Overall appearance softer and less crisp than adults, often with a faint gape and more hesitant movements.
description.in_flight: Flight typically consists of short, direct bursts between perches, with shallow wingbeats and a slightly undulating path. In flight, the white tips to the tail and the red vent can stand out, especially when the tail is fanned on landing.

behaviour: Usually seen in pairs or small family groups, often perched conspicuously on wires, shrub tops and exposed branches. Highly vocal, with a variety of ringing whistles, chattering notes and characteristic repeated phrases, often delivered from a prominent song perch. Frequently flicks tail and adjusts crest while calling. Forages in shrubs and small trees for fruit and insects, and also descends to the ground for fallen fruit. Nests are shallow cup nests placed in bushes or small trees, often close to human habitation. Bold and adaptable around people, commonly seen in gardens and plantations throughout the Sahyadris.
food: Primarily fruits and berries (including figs and cultivated fruits); also takes insects, small invertebrates, flower buds and nectar. Often feeds in mixed flocks at fruiting trees.

habitat: Tall evergreen and semi-evergreen forests, forest edges, mid- to upper-canopy layers. Often follows mixed-species bird flocks and frequents clearings within dense forests.

description.adult_male: Glossy black, highly iridescent in sunlight with bluish-green sheen. Prominent recurved crest on forehead. Extremely long tail streamers ending in distinctive spoon-shaped 'rackets'—the species’ signature feature. Strong, slightly hooked bill and bright red eyes.
description.adult_female: Similar to male, with slightly duller gloss and often shorter or narrower tail streamers. Crest may appear less pronounced.
description.juvenile: Duller sooty black with little or no iridescence. Crest absent or minimal. Tail streamers short or undeveloped; 'rackets' not yet formed. Iris dark brown.
description.in_flight: Long tail streamers trail dramatically, twisting during turns. Flashy silhouette with broad wings and characteristic undulating flight through canopy gaps.

behaviour: A bold, intelligent forest bird. Famous for exceptional mimicry—copies alarm calls, songs, and even mammal noises. Regular leader species in mixed hunting flocks, using its mimicked alarm calls to influence flock movement. Feeds actively by sallying for insects from exposed canopy perches or following mammals for disturbed prey. Aggressive towards predators and larger birds; known to chase raptors from territory. Crest raised when excited or during vocal displays.
food: Large flying insects, beetles, winged termites, small lizards, nectar, and occasionally fledglings or small birds. Often captures insects in aerial sallies.

habitat: Open scrub, cultivated fields, hedgerows, forest edges, dry grasslands and semi-open areas with scattered bushes or small trees. Frequently uses human-modified landscapes.

description.adult_male: Ashy-grey crown and nape, striking black mask through eye to ear coverts, rich rufous back and rump, and a bold white wing patch. Underparts pale buff. Long black tail with white outer edges. Variation across subspecies: some populations show blacker heads or deeper rufous mantles.
description.adult_female: Similar patterning but browner and duller overall. Mask reduced and less sharply defined. Underparts more buff and lightly barred.
description.juvenile: Warm buff-brown with fine barring on upperparts and flanks. Weak mask. Shorter tail, yellowish gape. Gradual loss of barring with age.
description.in_flight: White wing patch and rufous mantle flash distinctly. Flight low and direct with rapid wingbeats; often glides between exposed perches.

behaviour: Bold and highly territorial. Hunts from exposed perches such as wires, poles, or prominent twigs. Classic shrike behaviour includes impaling prey on thorns or barbed wire ('larders'), used for storage and mating displays. Calls include clear whistles, harsh 'shak-shak', chatters and scolding notes. Pairs defend territories vigorously, especially in breeding season. Frequently patrols open fields for prey.
food: Large insects, grasshoppers, beetles, caterpillars, lizards, small rodents, and occasionally small birds. Uses perch-to-ground sallying and ambush techniques.

habitat: Tall grasses in marshes, wet meadows, paddy margins, open country near water, and seasonally wet grasslands. Usually stays low but emerges to sing.

description.adult_male: Very small brown warbler. Breeding plumage: bright golden-orange crown contrasting with brown streaked upperparts, short rounded tail, and creamy underparts. Non-breeding male: crown dull brown and heavily streaked; tail becomes longer, giving a different silhouette.
description.adult_female: Resembles non-breeding male: less bright crown, longer tail, and slightly paler below. Subtler overall colours.
description.juvenile: Buff and brown with streaked upperparts; lacks bright golden crown. Underparts warm buff with diffuse streaks. Appears softer-toned and more rounded.
description.in_flight: Short, fluttery flight above grass-tops. Breeding males rise briefly for display flights, hovering with buzzing calls; short tail makes the bird appear more compact.

behaviour: Skulking but becomes conspicuous when singing. Males sing repetitive 'teewip' or buzzing notes from exposed grass stems. Performs display flights—spiralling upward then parachuting down while singing. Forages low within grasses, picking insects from stems. Builds elaborate sewn nests using grass and spider silk; dome-shaped with side entrance.
food: Small insects and larvae, spiders and other invertebrates gleaned from tall grass stems and leaves.

habitat: Open scrub, dry deciduous forest, secondary woodland, village edges, gardens, orchards and parks. Readily adapts to semi-urban and agricultural landscapes.

description.adult_male: Dull brownish-grey overall with a slightly streaked forehead and throat, pale greyish underparts, and a stout yellowish bill. Iris pale yellow to whitish, giving a 'staring' expression. Feathers loosely arranged, giving a scruffy appearance typical of the species.
description.adult_female: Indistinguishable from male in the field; sexes share identical plumage, size and behaviour.
description.juvenile: Fluffier and paler than adults with less defined streaking, duller grey-brown tone and a bright yellow gape. Iris darker than adults. Juveniles stay close to the group, following adults and giving persistent food-begging calls.
description.in_flight: Short, low, and noisy flights with wings and tail fanned. Typical movement is a series of fluttering hops between bushes or along the ground. Flocks keep tightly together while calling.

behaviour: Exceptionally social, usually found in cohesive groups of 6–10+ birds, giving rise to the nickname 'Seven Sisters'. Engages in cooperative breeding: several group members assist in incubation, feeding nestlings and defending territories. Frequent allopreening and allogrooming reinforce social bonds. Forages mostly on the ground, turning over leaf litter for insects and fallen fruits. Sentinels take elevated positions and give rasping alarm calls. Constant contact chatter keeps the flock coordinated. Roosts communally in dense shrubbery or low trees.
food: Wide-ranging diet including insects, beetles, ants, termites, caterpillars, seeds, berries, small fruits and occasional human food scraps. Will opportunistically take small lizards or frogs when available.

photos.caption: A Jungle Babbler perched at Mangaon, Raigad.
Individual from Kawal Wildlife Sanctuary, Andhra Pradesh.
A pair interacting after monsoon rain in Chalakudy, Kerala.

habitat: Flowering gardens, orchards, hedges, groves, scrub, open woodland, forest edges, coffee plantations and urban parks. Closely associated with flowering shrubs and trees including hibiscus, bottlebrush and ornamental species.

description.adult_male: Very small, intensely coloured sunbird with iridescent metallic plumage. Crown, mantle and sides of head glossy green; throat and chest deep maroon; shoulder patch bright metallic purple. Rump a vivid purplish-crimson. Belly pale yellow; undertail coverts whitish. Distinctive lemon-yellow pectoral tufts visible when excited or displaying. Bill thin, decurved and black.
description.adult_female: Much duller than male: upperparts olive-brown; underparts clean yellow with whitish throat; faint eyebrow; wings brownish. Tail dark with white outer tail shafts sometimes visible in flight.
description.juvenile: Resembles the adult female but even duller; brownish-olive above and washed-out yellow below. Bill shorter and paler at base. Lacks the clean contrasts of adults and shows faint gape flanges. Young males begin showing dull maroon tones on throat as they mature.
description.in_flight: Fast, buzzing, and highly agile flight with rapid wingbeats. Frequently darts between flowers in short bursts. Capable of brief hovering, though usually perches to feed. In backlit conditions the male’s purple rump and green crown flash conspicuously.

behaviour: Restless, energetic and constantly moving among flowers. Often hangs upside down to reach deep corollas. Males display by fluffing yellow pectoral tufts and singing from exposed perches. Aggressive around rich nectar sources; frequently chases other sunbirds and even larger birds. At the nest, female performs most building and incubation while the male guards territory. Nest is a delicate hanging purse with a side entrance, woven from plant fibres, cobwebs and lichens. Vocalisations include sharp 'chip' notes and a rapid metallic twittering.
food: Primarily nectar from flowering shrubs and trees; also takes insects, small spiders and caterpillars, especially during breeding to feed young. Opportunistically gleans insects from foliage.

habitat: Forest clearings, edges of moist and dry deciduous forests, open cultivation, orchards, plantations, towns and villages. More associated with wooded habitats than Common Myna.

description.adult_male: Greyish-brown overall with darker head and breast. Distinctive drooping tuft of feathers at the base of the bill. Bill and legs bright yellow. Tail blackish with white tips visible in flight. Iris pale yellow to whitish.
description.adult_female: Indistinguishable from male in most field conditions; same grey-brown plumage, facial tuft and yellow bill/legs.
description.juvenile: Duller brown, less pronounced bill-base tuft, darker legs, and a brownish iris. Underparts more uniformly brown and softer-toned.
description.in_flight: Shows white tail tips and dark wings; flight strong and somewhat bounding when moving between perches or fields.

behaviour: Highly social and often noisy. Forms large communal roosts in trees, sometimes mixed with other mynas or starlings. Walks confidently on the ground rather than hopping. Often forages in groups in fields, livestock areas and garbage dumps. Uses a variety of calls—chirps, whistles, chatters—and mimics other birds occasionally. Opportunistic breeder using tree cavities, buildings or nest boxes.
food: Omnivorous: insects, fruit, grains, kitchen scraps, small invertebrates, flower nectar. Frequently follows ploughing to capture disturbed insects.

habitat: Open country, agricultural fields, forest edges, dry grasslands, rocky cliffs, road cuttings, bridges, culverts and buildings—especially where suitable ledges for mud nests exist.

description.adult_male: Glossy deep blue upperparts with a rich rufous collar and a warm red-brown rump. Underparts off-white to buff with heavy dark streaking. Long, slightly forked tail with white wedges on outer feathers visible in flight.
description.adult_female: Similar to male with only slightly duller gloss. Plumage pattern and streaking nearly identical.
description.juvenile: Paler and duller with less distinct rufous collar and weaker streaking below. Tail shorter with less pronounced white wedges.
description.in_flight: Fast, graceful flier with long glides and quick wingbeats. White tail patches and rufous rump are striking. Often flies low over fields, water bodies, and roads.

behaviour: Highly aerial; spends most of time catching insects on the wing. Calls include soft twittering and chirpy notes. Builds distinctive enclosed mud nests with narrow entrance tubes on vertical surfaces—bridges and culverts commonly used in the Ghats. Migratory populations appear during winter and early summer, often joining mixed swallow flocks.
food: Flying insects: flies, winged termites, beetles, small moths. Feeds low over ground, fields or water.

habitat: Shady forest floor, leaf litter, wet undergrowth, bamboo thickets, coffee plantations, shrubby ravines and riverine forest. Prefers moist wooded areas during wintering.

description.adult_male: Olive-brown above with clean white underparts and two bold black breast bands. Face with pale supercilium. Wings show dark coverts with pale edging. Tail long and blackish with white outer feathers. Distinctive lateral (side-to-side) tail-wagging behaviour.
description.adult_female: Similar to male, sometimes with slightly narrower breast bands but generally identical in field appearance.
description.juvenile: Browner and duller with incomplete or washed-out breast bands. Tail slightly shorter; lateral wagging already present but less pronounced.
description.in_flight: Low, fluttery flight through undergrowth; white outer tail feathers noticeable. Frequently flushes from leaf litter with a sharp call.

behaviour: A solitary winter visitor that walks briskly on the forest floor—rarely seen in open areas. Its lateral tail-wagging is unique among wagtails. Feeds quietly among leaf litter, often in shaded, humid patches. When alarmed, flies up to a low branch before dropping back down. Gives soft 'tsip' calls. Often returns to the same wintering patches year after year.
food: Small insects, ants, beetles, spiders, and other ground-dwelling invertebrates gleaned from leaf litter.

habitat: Dry scrub, open grasslands, fallow fields, rocky plateaus, sparse Acacia scrub, edges of cultivation. Prefers warm, open terrain with scattered bushes.

description.adult_male: Warm brown, heavily streaked above with dark centers to the feathers. Buffy underparts with fine streaking on the breast. Short, rounded tail. Rufous wing panel noticeable when perched and especially in flight. Buff supercilium and strong bill typical of bushlarks.
description.adult_female: Indistinguishable from the male; plumage and wing patches identical.
description.juvenile: Softer brown overall with more diffuse streaking and paler edges to the feathers; less pronounced rufous wing patch; gape pale yellowish.
description.in_flight: Shows rounded wings with rich rufous patches, short tail, and fluttery, bounding flight during song display. Calls and sings while ascending high into the air.

behaviour: Mostly terrestrial, walking or running quickly between grass clumps. Territorial males perform a high, fluttering song-flight—rising vertically, singing continuously, then parachuting down. Often perches on stones, clods, or small bushes to deliver call notes. Mostly solitary or in loose pairs.
food: Seeds, grass seeds, ants, beetles, small insects; diet shifts to more insects during breeding season.

habitat: Dry and moist deciduous forests, mixed woodland, forest edges, teak plantations, large groves. Prefers tall trees and mid-to-upper canopy.

description.adult_male: Glossy black head, neck, and upper breast contrasting with pale grey back and upperparts. Wings dark with pale edging; underparts white to pale grey. Tail dark with white tips visible in flight.
description.adult_female: Grey replaces the male’s black; overall paler with less contrast. Underparts whitish with faint barring or wash.
description.juvenile: Brownish-grey above with faint scaling and mottling; head not fully dark; underparts lightly streaked.
description.in_flight: Slow, slightly undulating flight with white tail edges noticeable. Often moves from tree to tree at mid-canopy height.

behaviour: Frequently solitary or in pairs; sometimes joins mixed-hunting flocks in winter. Methodical canopy forager—gleaning caterpillars and insects from leaves and twigs. Gives soft whistles and harsh notes. Males sit upright when calling. Moves quietly through foliage and is easy to miss unless calling.
food: Caterpillars, beetles, mantises, various insects; occasionally small fruits.

habitat: Open broadleaf woodland, groves, orchards, riverine forest, gardens, large fruiting trees. Prefers tall, leafy canopy.

description.adult_male: Brilliant golden-yellow plumage, contrasting black eye-stripe and black wings with yellow patches. Bill pinkish-red. Clean yellow tail with black central shafts.
description.adult_female: Greener-olive above, yellowish below with grey or olive streaking on the breast. Eye-stripe weaker. Wings browner.
description.juvenile: Similar to adult female but more streaked below, duller above, and with brownish bill.
description.in_flight: Strong, direct flight with loud flapping; yellow body with contrasting black wings striking against the canopy.

behaviour: Secretive, staying high in leafy canopies. Despite its colour, it often remains hidden. Gives rich fluting whistles—its classic ‘wee-loo’ song carrying far. Moves through branches picking fruit and insects. Sometimes territorial during breeding; nests are cup-shaped slings placed between forked branches.
food: Figs, berries, soft fruits, insects, caterpillars; occasionally raids bird nests for eggs or chicks.

habitat: Moist deciduous forests, evergreen forests, forest edges, plantations with tall canopy. Prefers mid-to-upper canopy.

description.adult_male: Brilliant grass-green overall. Yellow throat blending into green breast. Bold black mask and bib around bill and face. Wings dark with green edging; tail green with darker center.
description.adult_female: Similar green plumage but throat greener (not bright yellow) and black mask reduced or absent. Overall softer appearance.
description.juvenile: Entirely green without mask; throat plain. Bill duller and shorter.
description.in_flight: Fast, darting flight across the canopy; green body often blends with foliage.

behaviour: Active and restless canopy forager, hopping among leaves, gleaning insects and nectar. Highly vocal and capable of remarkable mimicry of other birds. Often part of mixed flocks. Territorial males sing rich whistles from mid-canopy positions.
food: Nectar, small insects, caterpillars, berries; frequently visits flowering trees such as Bombax and Erythrina.

habitat: Open woodland, gardens, orchards, shrubland, groves, roadside trees. Often in low to mid canopy.

description.adult_male: Bright yellow underparts, greenish back, and black wings with two bold white bars. Breeding males develop black cap, mantle and back, contrasting sharply with yellow underparts.
description.adult_female: Greener overall with olive back and wings; underparts yellowish but paler and more diffuse than male. Wingbars present but less crisp.
description.juvenile: Duller greenish-yellow with less distinct wingbars; underparts washed and unstreaked.
description.in_flight: Flits rapidly through foliage; white wingbars visible in bursts of short flights.

behaviour: Active, acrobatic forager often seen gleaning insects from leaves or hanging upside-down. Frequently in pairs with constant soft contact calls. Breeding males perform dramatic swinging and twisted courtship displays while singing rich, variable whistles. Builds neat, cup-shaped nests suspended from forked branches.
food: Spiders, caterpillars, beetles, small insects; occasionally small fruit and nectar.

habitat: Open country, grasslands, paddy fields, sugarcane, village edges, reedbeds, and areas near water. Prefers regions with tall grasses suitable for nest-building.

description.adult_male: In breeding plumage, bright yellow crown and forehead, yellow breast, dark brown streaked upperparts and black mask. Non-breeding males resemble females: buff-brown above, pale below, faint streaking, and no bright yellow.
description.adult_female: Sparrow-like; warm brown above with streaks, pale underparts, faint supercilium, conical bill.
description.juvenile: Buffier and paler than females, with weaker streaking and a yellowish gape. Bill is pale horn-coloured.
description.in_flight: Short, fluttery flight; in breeding males the yellow crown is conspicuous. Gregarious flocks move low over grasslands in bounding flight.

behaviour: A highly colonial species famous for its intricately woven, pendulous nests made of grass strips. Males build multiple 'test' nests and perform display flights to attract females, who inspect and choose preferred nests. Loud, buzzing 'chee-chee' calls around colonies. Feeds in flocks, often mixing with munias and other weavers. Very active and vocal during the monsoon breeding season.
food: Grass seeds, paddy, millet, grain, small insects—especially during breeding.

habitat: Grasslands, scrub, paddy fields, sugarcane, gardens, canalsides and open farmland. Prefers dense grass clumps for roosting.

description.adult_male: Dark chocolate-brown above; underparts white with fine vertical streaking. Bold white rump visible in flight. Black mask and throat patch. Short, thick blue-grey bill typical of munias.
description.adult_female: Plumage identical to male; sexes not separable in the field.
description.juvenile: Warm buff-brown overall, lacking adult streaking. Throat brown, bill pale greyish, rump duller. Often seen begging in post-breeding flocks.
description.in_flight: Tight flocking flight, fast and direct. The bright white rump is striking when birds flush together.

behaviour: Extremely gregarious; moves in compact flocks that shift rapidly between grass patches and cultivated fields. Constant soft chipping calls. Frequently clings acrobatically to grass stalks to feed on seeds. Roosts communally and often nests in colonies within thick vegetation.
food: Grass seeds (primary), paddy grains, small insects (especially during breeding).

habitat: Forests, wooded hills, forest edges, coffee estates, and open woodland adjoining dense greenery. Often associated with valleys and ridges of the Western Ghats.

description.adult_male: Prominent erect crest; upperparts dark brown; underparts white with heavy brown streaking on breast and belly. Powerful yellow eyes; strong, barred wings; long, banded tail.
description.adult_female: Similar to male but larger and heavier-bodied; otherwise identical plumage.
description.juvenile: Whitish head and body with sparse streaking; crest shorter or absent; underparts buffier; eyes paler.
description.in_flight: Long, broad wings held in a slight dihedral while soaring. Tail well-banded. Appears powerful and slow-flapping, often soaring high over ridges.

behaviour: A dominant forest raptor that soars over canopy edges or perches high to scan for prey. Hunts birds, large lizards (monitors), snakes, squirrels and occasionally young monkeys. Calls include high, penetrating whistles. Pairs hold large territories. Builds substantial stick nests on large trees.
food: Small to medium-sized mammals, birds, lizards, snakes.

habitat: Cliffs, escarpments of the Western Ghats, rocky outcrops, coasts, open plains, and occasionally urban high-rises. Prefers exposed ledges for roosting.

description.adult_male: Slate-grey upperparts, finely barred white underparts, bold black hood and moustachial stripe. Yellow cere and eye-ring. Compact, muscular build.
description.adult_female: Similar but much larger and bulkier than male; barring coarser on breast.
description.juvenile: Brown above with buff edges, heavily streaked below instead of barred; facial pattern browner and less sharp.
description.in_flight: Long, pointed wings and long tail. Extremely fast, agile flight. Streamlined silhouette with sickle-shaped wings.

behaviour: Famous for its high-speed stoop, reaching over 300 km/h. Hunts birds in mid-air—doves, pigeons, waders, smaller raptors. Often perches on high cliffs overlooking valleys. Mostly solitary. Wintering individuals remain faithful to seasonal territories. Calls include harsh 'kek-kek-kek' alarms.
food: Primarily birds; occasionally bats.

habitat: Open country, grasslands, agricultural fields, forest edges, fallow land, wetlands and lakesides. Often seen hunting over termite swarms and cultivated areas. Prefers open skies and scattered trees or wires for perching.

description.adult_male: Small, slender falcon. Uniform dark slate-grey upperparts and underparts, giving a smooth charcoal appearance. Rufous vent and thighs distinctive. Face clean with reduced moustachial stripe; dark eyes. Tail long and slightly rounded. Wings narrow and pointed.
description.adult_female: Very different from male. Whitish underparts with heavy dark streaking; wings and back brownish with darker centres; pale face with strong dark moustachial stripe; orange-yellow orbital skin. Tail barred. Rufous vent less bright than male.
description.juvenile: Brown upperparts with pale buff edges, strongly streaked below similar to adult female but duller. Facial pattern softer; bill pale. Shows buffy fringes on upperwing coverts.
description.in_flight: Fast, buoyant wingbeats. Long pointed wings reminiscent of hobbies. Often hovers briefly while hunting. Males appear very dark below in flight; females show streaked underside. Frequently seen in flocks during migration.

behaviour: Highly migratory; passes through India in huge numbers en route from Mongolia/China to southern Africa. Feeds aerially, often gathering in large flocks over termite emergences. Hunts insects in swift, acrobatic chases. Frequently perches on wires in small flocks. Roosts communally during migration in tall trees or reedbeds. Calls are soft 'kek-kek' notes, but generally silent in wintering grounds.
food: Insects—especially termites, dragonflies, grasshoppers, and beetles. Occasionally takes small vertebrates.

habitat: Lakes, marshes, estuaries, rivers, flooded paddy fields and mangroves. Prefers shallow wetlands and edges of standing water.

description.adult_male: Slender all-white egret with long black bill, black legs and bright yellow feet. During breeding displays two long, delicate nape plumes ('aigrettes'), fine breast plumes, and flushed reddish lores.
description.adult_female: Similar to male; sexes indistinguishable in the field. Same white plumage and breeding plumes.
description.juvenile: Duller, lacking head plumes; lores greyish and bill slightly paler. Legs duller with reduced yellow on feet.
description.in_flight: White bird with long trailing black legs and feet; slow, graceful wingbeats. Neck retracted in typical heron posture.

behaviour: Active hunter, often runs, dashes and shuffles its brightly coloured feet to flush prey. Also uses 'stand-and-wait' strategy. Frequently seen alone or in loose groups. Roosts communally with other egrets and herons.
food: Small fish, crustaceans, aquatic insects, frogs, tadpoles.

habitat: Marshes, ponds, paddy fields, roadside ditches, wet scrub and village tanks. Favors dense vegetation close to water.

description.adult_male: Slate-grey to dark grey body contrasting with pure white face, throat and breast. Rump and vent bright chestnut-red. Legs long, greenish-yellow. Bill yellow with red base.
description.adult_female: Same plumage as male; no reliable differences.
description.juvenile: Browner plumage, dingier face, reduced contrast between grey and white areas. Vent duller.
description.in_flight: Short, weak flight with dangling legs; wings show broad rounded shape and dark-and-white contrast.

behaviour: Bold and noisy. Often emerges from cover, flicking tail rapidly to reveal chestnut vent. Loud, repetitive 'kroo-kroo' calls especially at dusk. Walks confidently on floating vegetation. Territorial pairs year-round.
food: Insects, snails, small fish, worms, seeds and algae.

habitat: Marshes, large tanks, flooded fields, freshwater wetlands. Prefers shallow, snail-rich waterbodies.

description.adult_male: Pale grey-white stork with glossy black flight feathers. Distinctive gap between the closed mandibles, adapted for extracting snails. Bill heavy and yellowish. Non-breeding adults appear greyer.
description.adult_female: Like male; sexes identical.
description.juvenile: Browner overall with duller plumage; bill greyish without strong gap pronounced early on.
description.in_flight: Strong, steady wingbeats. Shows black flight feathers and broad wings; neck outstretched as in all storks.

behaviour: Feeds by probing and manipulating snails using the specialised bill gap. Often forages in groups. Soars on thermals when commuting between wetlands. Roosts colonially in tall trees.
food: Aquatic snails (especially *Pila* species), frogs, molluscs, large insects.

habitat: Shallow ponds, lakes, marshes, paddy fields and irrigation canals; prefers still or slow-moving water with vegetation.

description.adult_male: Warm rufous body, dark cap, long graceful neck. Shoulders show bold dark-and-rufous patterns. Eyes pale golden-yellow.
description.adult_female: Similar to male, sexes identical in field.
description.juvenile: Duller brown with less contrast on wings; underparts buffier.
description.in_flight: Shows rounded wings and warm rufous body; calls a soft, descending whistling note while flying.

behaviour: Often seen in tight flocks. Diurnal loafers and nocturnal feeders. Frequently perches on branches over water. Very tolerant of human presence and commonly seen in village tanks.
food: Aquatic vegetation, seeds, rice grains, small molluscs and aquatic insects.

habitat: Large rivers, reservoirs, lakes, estuaries and coastal wetlands. Prefers deeper inland waters than Little Cormorant.

description.adult_male: Slim dark cormorant with long narrow bill and sharp hook. Breeding plumage shows white streaks on neck, pale facial skin and bluish gape. Head and neck appear more elongated than Little Cormorant.
description.adult_female: Similar to male; sexes identical.
description.juvenile: Browner and duller with paler throat; lacks breeding streaking.
description.in_flight: Fast and direct flight with powerful wingbeats; long neck stretched out. Flies in loose lines over water.

behaviour: Dives gracefully and swims low in water with only head and neck visible. Often perches on snags, spreading wings to dry. Breeds colonially with other waterbirds.
food: Fish primarily; occasionally aquatic insects and crustaceans.

habitat: Small ponds, village tanks, marshes, irrigation canals and ditches with abundant emergent vegetation.

description.adult_male: Small, rounded waterbird with very short tail (often invisible). Breeding plumage features rich rufous neck and cheeks, dark brown body, contrasting pale gape patch. Bill short and dark.
description.adult_female: Like male; sexes are not separable in the field. Same rufous neck during breeding season.
description.juvenile: Brownish overall with pale stripes on face and neck. Duller and less rufous than adults.
description.in_flight: Very rarely seen flying; weak fluttering flight with trailing legs. Usually avoids flight by diving.

behaviour: Extremely aquatic, seldom comes ashore. Dives instantly when disturbed and may stay submerged for long periods. Eats its own feathers and feeds feathers to chicks—an adaptation to protect the stomach from fish bones. Builds floating nests in reeds. Quiet but gives sharp, trilling calls during breeding.
food: Aquatic insects, larvae, small fish, tadpoles and crustaceans.

habitat: Rocky streams, riverbanks, lakeshores, reservoirs, mangroves and small forest streams.

description.adult_male: Brownish-grey above with fine streaking; white below with characteristic upcurving breast patches. Distinctive teetering (bobbing) motion while walking.
description.adult_female: Very similar; sexes not distinguishable in field.
description.juvenile: Fresher-edged wing feathers, buffy fringes, less crisp patterning; retains teetering habit.
description.in_flight: Shows a sharp white wingbar and dark upperparts; flies low over water with rapid, shallow wingbeats.

behaviour: Usually solitary and highly active along water edges. Constant tail/body bobbing while foraging is diagnostic. Often flushes with a sharp 'twee-wee-wee' call. Winters widely across peninsular India.
food: Aquatic insects, crustaceans, worms and small molluscs.

habitat: Open fields, dry riverbeds, cultivation, grasslands and edges of waterbodies. Frequently near villages.

description.adult_male: Black crown, face, throat and chest; white belly and flanks; brown wings and back. Bright red wattle in front of eye, red bill with black tip, and long yellow legs.
description.adult_female: Same as male; no difference in plumage.
description.juvenile: Browner with duller face pattern and buff-edged feathers. Wattles reduced or absent.
description.in_flight: Shows bold black-and-white wings; loud calls while flying. Strong, straight flight.

behaviour: Extremely vocal and alert. Famous call resembles 'Did-he-do-it?' or 'Tit-tit-dit!'. Ground nester; bravely defends nesting area with distraction displays—feigning injury to lure predators away. Often active at night around wetlands.
food: Insects, beetles, ants, earthworms and small invertebrates.

habitat: Scrub, dense undergrowth, forest edges, agricultural fields, bamboo groves and gardens. Often near human habitation.

description.adult_male: Large cuckoo with glossy black head/body, rich chestnut wings and very long black tail. Eyes deep ruby-red. Heavy, arched bill.
description.adult_female: Same overall appearance; females sometimes slightly browner.
description.juvenile: Browner body with buff barring on wings and tail; duller eye colour.
description.in_flight: Heavy, floppy flight with broad wings; chestnut wings show clearly when flying short distances.

behaviour: Solitary, slow-moving and skulking. Gives deep, booming 'coop-coop-coop' or 'hoop-hoop' calls—often at dawn and during monsoon. Poor flier; prefers running and clambering through vegetation. Known to take nestlings of other birds.
food: Large insects, caterpillars, snails, small reptiles, frogs, bird eggs and nestlings.

habitat: Wetlands, fields, gardens, forest edges, mangroves and open country. Often far from water, unlike most kingfishers.

description.adult_male: Rich chestnut brown head and underparts, bright turquoise-blue wings and back, contrasting white throat and breast, and large red bill.
description.adult_female: Same plumage as male; sexes impossible to separate in the field.
description.juvenile: Duller, with brownish-edged wing feathers and paler bill.
description.in_flight: Shows brilliant blue wing panels; strong, direct flight with rapid wingbeats.

behaviour: One of the commonest kingfishers of the region. Hunts from exposed perches, diving for prey or hawking insects in flight. Loud, rattling 'kek-kek-kek' call. Highly territorial and active throughout the day.
food: Fish, frogs, large insects, lizards, small snakes and occasionally small rodents.

habitat: Evergreen, semi-evergreen, and moist deciduous forests of the Western Ghats; prefers tall forest with fruiting trees, especially figs.

description.adult_male: Large grey hornbill with long tail; pale grey head streaked white; dark grey-brown body. Bill long and curved, dull yellowish with a pale base; casque narrow and inconspicuous. Males typically have a brighter yellow bill and slightly larger casque.
description.adult_female: Similar to male but bill often duller, with more orange-brown or horn-coloured tones; casque slightly smaller. Head streaking identical.
description.juvenile: Duller grey overall; bill smaller and pale horn-coloured with minimal casque. Lacks crisp head streaking.
description.in_flight: Strong, steady wingbeats; wings appear broad and rounded. Tail long and dark with pale tip visible in good light.

behaviour: Highly arboreal and strongly frugivorous, moving in small groups through mid- to upper canopy. Loud, cackling calls echo through forests at dawn and dusk. Nests in tree cavities; female seals herself inside with a mud-and-food-pulp wall, leaving only a slit for feeding. Plays a key ecological role as a seed disperser of large forest fruits. Populations are declining due to habitat fragmentation across the Western Ghats.
food: Primarily figs and other large fruits; also insects, small reptiles and occasional small vertebrates.

habitat: Evergreen and moist deciduous forests, wooded valleys, orchards, plantations and well-treed cultivation; also visits fruiting trees in villages.

description.adult_male: Striking parakeet with rich plum-coloured (pinkish-purple) head, narrow black chin stripe and black neck ring. Green body with bluish tail and bright blue rump. Red upper mandible, pale lower mandible.
description.adult_female: Head duller and more greyish-blue, lacking the black neck ring; bill entirely blackish. Body green with similar blue rump.
description.juvenile: Both sexes green-headed with only a hint of bluish wash; no neck ring; bill pale horn-coloured.
description.in_flight: Fast, direct flight with rapid wingbeats; long tail streamers and blue rump stand out.

behaviour: Noisy, fast-moving flocks, often at canopy height. Frequently mixes with other parakeets at fruiting trees. Calls include sharp 'twit-twit' notes and rolling chatter. Not endemic to the Western Ghats—range covers much of peninsular India and Himalayan foothills. Distinguished from the true Western Ghats endemic, the Malabar Parakeet, by its purple head and lack of greenish-blue head.
food: Fruits, seeds, berries, leaf buds, nectar; raids crops occasionally.

habitat: Dry and moist deciduous forest edges, scrub-jungle, farmland, riverine belts and village outskirts. Avoids deep forest interior.

description.adult_male: Magnificent iridescent blue neck and breast; long shimmering train of elongated tail-coverts with 'eye' patterns. Crest fan-shaped. Wings dark chestnut with white patches. Train length highly variable.
description.adult_female: Brown and white, lacking train. Greenish sheen on neck; crest smaller and brownish. Overall slimmer and smaller.
description.juvenile: Duller brown; young males begin showing metallic neck sheen early but lack train.
description.in_flight: Heavy, powerful flight; broad, rounded wings; train trails behind and is not aerodynamic, so males avoid long flights.

behaviour: Ground-feeding omnivore, often near water and cultivation. Males display extravagant train-fanning ('courtship fan') mainly during monsoon breeding. Loud 'kee-ow' calls echo at dawn and dusk. Roosts on tall trees. Generally tolerant of human presence.
food: Seeds, grain, insects, small snakes, lizards, and berries.

habitat: Dense moist deciduous and evergreen forests with large trees; favours ravines, steep slopes and secluded valleys.

description.adult_male: Massive owl with prominent ear tufts, dark brown upperparts, bold black spotting on pale underparts, and large yellowish eyes. Broad, powerful head and deep hoot.
description.adult_female: Slightly larger than male but similar in plumage; sexes not easily distinguished.
description.juvenile: Fluffier white to creamy plumage with incomplete spotting; developing ear tufts.
description.in_flight: Large, broad-winged silhouette; slow, deep wingbeats; silent flight typical of owls.

behaviour: Solitary, strictly nocturnal predator. Roosts in dense foliage or shaded rock ledges during the day. Deep, booming calls carry long distances. Feeds on surprisingly large prey—including junglefowl, hares, large rodents and occasionally small mammals or other birds. Rare and easily overlooked in dense forest.
food: Large rodents, hares, junglefowl, pigeons, small mammals and occasionally reptiles.

habitat: Open areas, near water, farmlands, riverbeds.

description.adult_male: Bright green body, reddish throat, prominent black line through the eye, long central tail feathers.
description.adult_female: Bright green body, reddish throat, prominent black line through the eye, long central tail feathers.

behaviour: Perches high, often wires, darts out to catch insects in flight. Forms large communal roosts.
food: Bees, wasps, flying insects.

habitat: Open agricultural fields, grasslands, forest edges, scrubland, village outskirts and along roadsides. Prefers open habitats with scattered perches such as wires, poles or bare trees.

description.adult_male: Striking combination of vivid blues and warm browns. Crown and nape pale turquoise-blue; mantle and breast warm rufous-brown; throat lilac-washed. Wings extremely bright with deep ultramarine, turquoise and purple-blue panels. Rump bright blue; tail short with bluish tip. Bill strong and dark; eyes pale brown.
description.adult_female: Sexes alike, with identical coloration and pattern; females may be marginally duller but not reliably distinguishable in the field.
description.juvenile: Duller overall with browner upperparts and less saturated blues. Underparts buffy and plain; wing panels lack full brilliance of adults. Throat and breast less lilac-toned; flight feathers narrower and less contrasting.
description.in_flight: Highly distinctive flashing bands of blue and purple on the wings. Underwing shows three contrasting bars: deep blue, pale blue and purple. Flight strong and purposeful, alternating with sudden swoops. During display the bird performs exaggerated rolls, dives and loops, revealing the bright turquoise patches.

behaviour: Typically seen perched conspicuously on wires, poles or bare branches, scanning the ground for prey. Hunts by dropping from a perch in a rapid glide to seize insects or small vertebrates. Frequently active near roadsides where insects are abundant. During the breeding season performs its famous 'rolling' aerial display with tumbling, diving loops accompanied by harsh calls. Territorial and vocal; emits repeated rasping 'chack-chack' notes while displaying.
food: Large insects such as grasshoppers, crickets and beetles; also feeds on small reptiles, frogs, large termites and occasionally small rodents.

photos.caption: Adult perched on roadside wire showing typical bright turquoise crown and rufous breast.
Individual in flight with vivid ultramarine and purple wing panels.

habitat: Deciduous and evergreen forests, bamboo clumps.

description.adult_male: Unmistakable rufous-brown plumage with black barring on the flanks and wings. Male has a small red cheek patch.
description.adult_female: Unmistakable rufous-brown plumage with black barring on the flanks and wings.

behaviour: Forages mainly on ant-ridden trees; unique in using arboreal ant nests to build its own nest chamber.
food: Ants and termites.

habitat: Moist deciduous and evergreen forests, fruiting trees.

description.adult_male: Overall green body, brown crown and cheek, distinct white streaking on the throat and cheeks.
description.adult_female: Overall green body, brown crown and cheek, distinct white streaking on the throat and cheeks.

behaviour: Arboreal, often heard but rarely seen; known for its monotonous, loud `ku-trook, ku-trook` call.
food: Fruits (figs are favored), flowers, insects.

habitat: Grasslands, scrub, cultivation (often hides in dense cover).

description.adult_male: Small, quail-like bird; yellowish legs, mottled rufous and black upperparts. Females are brighter and larger.
description.adult_female: Small, quail-like bird; yellowish legs, mottled rufous and black upperparts. Females are brighter and larger.

behaviour: Terrestrial, runs quickly to escape; the female is polyandrous and uses a low, soft booming call to attract mates.
food: Seeds and insects.

habitat: Montane evergreen forests (shola) and adjacent moist hill forests above 600m.

description.adult_male: Slaty blue body, brighter blue on the forehead and rump. Distinctive white patch at the base of the tail visible in flight.
description.adult_female: Duller blue-grey overall, less distinct white tail patch.
description.juvenile: Brownish with buff spotting; tail patch hinted.

behaviour: Perches upright and sallies out to catch insects; generally silent outside the breeding season.
food: Insects.

habitat: Dense undergrowth and stream banks in high-elevation shola forests.

description.adult_male: Unmistakable glossy black head and upperparts contrasting sharply with brilliant orange underparts and rump.
description.adult_female: Similar to male but duller orange, and the black areas are brownish-black.
description.juvenile: Dull brown and scaled.

behaviour: Skulks in dense low vegetation, flicking its tail; mainly insectivorous.
food: Insects.

habitat: Moist evergreen and semi-evergreen forests, especially fruiting fig trees.

description.adult_male: Predominantly green body; distinctive red forehead and black/white streaks on the throat. Orange eye-ring.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, markings less distinct.

behaviour: Arboreal; known for its ringing, territorial 'kutruk-kutruk' call. Feeds mostly on fruit.
food: Fruits, especially figs; insects.

habitat: Forests, groves, and cultivated areas with berry-producing trees.

description.adult_male: Tiny, greyish-brown above, buffy-white below. Very short, stout bill. Plain plumage contrasts with other colourful flowerpeckers.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller overall.

behaviour: Highly active; feeds primarily on berries and figs, playing a crucial role in seed dispersal.
food: Berries, nectar, and small spiders.

habitat: Shola forests (forest patches in valleys of high-altitude grasslands); dense canopy and undergrowth above 1200m in Nilgiris, Bababudan Hills, Brahmagiris.

description.adult_male: Small, chunky bird with long legs and short tail. Dark slaty-blue upperparts, throat and breast. Warm rufous lower plumage, transitioning to buffy white at center of belly. Black lores, diffuse bluish brow.
description.adult_female: Similar to male, possibly with different iris color.
description.juvenile: Duller than adults.
description.in_flight: Short tail and chunky body conspicuous. Low, direct flight between dense vegetation.

behaviour: Skulking species found on forest floor and dark lower canopy. Can be confiding when observed. Calls frequently with tit-like notes and harsh rattles. Song consists of shrill whistles interspersed with twangy buzzing sounds.
food: Insects and invertebrates from forest floor and low vegetation.

habitat: Dense undergrowth and edges of high-altitude shola forests (South Western Ghats).

description.adult_male: Slaty-blue upperparts, black face, clean white belly and undertail coverts. Thin dark legs.
description.adult_female: Duller blue and slightly browner on the flanks.
description.juvenile: Brown with scaly patterning.

behaviour: Very secretive, foraging low on the ground and in dense vegetation; often only heard, singing a melodious territorial song.
food: Insects and small invertebrates.

habitat: Evergreen and moist deciduous forests, especially areas with fruiting trees.

description.adult_male: Olive-green overall with a lilac/pink patch on the mantle; grey cap and yellowish-green underparts.
description.adult_female: Duller, lacks the lilac patch on the mantle.
description.juvenile: Duller than female, greener head.
description.in_flight: Fast, direct flight; yellow feet visible.

behaviour: Arboreal and social, feeds in flocks high in the canopy on fruit.
food: Fruits and berries, especially figs.

habitat: Moist deciduous and evergreen forests with tall, old trees.

description.adult_male: Very large, glossy black woodpecker with a clean white belly and prominent red crest/mustache stripe.
description.adult_female: Similar, but red restricted to the hind-crown (no red mustache).
description.juvenile: Duller black, less extensive red.

behaviour: Drums loudly and powerfully on tall trees; solitary or in pairs, climbing trunks and large branches.
food: Wood-boring larvae, ants, beetles.

habitat: Dense, dark understorey of evergreen and moist deciduous forests.

description.adult_male: Black head, broad white breast band, brilliant scarlet-red belly/undertail. Golden-brown back.
description.adult_female: Chestnut head and breast, duller yellow-brown back, yellow belly.
description.juvenile: Duller and browner overall.

behaviour: Perches upright and motionless on horizontal branches; sallies out for insects, often in the twilight hours.
food: Insects, spiders, small lizards.

habitat: Dense undergrowth, forest floor of evergreen and deciduous forests.

description.adult_male: Brilliant and multicoloured: green wings, blue rump, black head/eye stripe, buff crown, bright red vent.
description.adult_female: Slightly duller but similar.
description.juvenile: Duller and browner.
description.in_flight: Shows flashing white patch in the wing.

behaviour: Shy and solitary, forages on the forest floor; calls loudly and clearly: 'wett-t-wett-t-tee' during the breeding season.
food: Insects, worms, grubs, and snails.

habitat: Dense reedbeds, scrub, and riparian vegetation along forest streams.

description.adult_male: Distinctive uniform grey head, olive-yellow body, and pale yellow undertail coverts.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, head brownish-grey.

behaviour: Skulks in dense cover; known for its characteristic high-pitched song and chattering calls.
food: Fruits, berries, and insects.

habitat: Forest undergrowth, dense bushes, wooded gardens.

description.adult_male: Dark grey/black body, prominent white throat patch, and a distinct white supercilium. Fanned tail with white edges.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Browner, less distinct markings.

behaviour: Hyperactive; constantly fans and closes its tail in a semi-circle while hopping through foliage, catching insects.
food: Insects and spiders.

habitat: Dry deciduous forests, open groves, roadside trees, and urban areas.

description.adult_male: Dull greyish-brown body, long tail, and a large blackish bill with a small casque/horn that is mainly black.
description.adult_female: Similar to male, with a smaller, duller casque.
description.juvenile: Smaller bill, no casque.

behaviour: Arboreal; feeds on fruit and insects; like the Malabar Grey Hornbill, it nests in sealed tree cavities.
food: Fruits, insects, small reptiles.

habitat: Dry scrub, stony hillsides, and dense forest undergrowth.

description.adult_male: Rufous-brown body with fine grey barring and white spots; grey head, red legs with multiple spurs.
description.adult_female: Duller, less spotted, lacks the prominent spurs.
description.in_flight: Short, rapid burst of flight before dropping into cover.

behaviour: Terrestrial; runs to escape rather than flies; often found in pairs; loud, cackling calls at dawn and dusk.
food: Seeds, berries, tubers, and insects.

habitat: Moist evergreen and deciduous forests; often seen near fruiting trees.

description.adult_male: Glossy black hood, grey back, white belly, and distinctive white patch in the wing, visible in flight.
description.adult_female: Similar to male, but duller grey where the male is black.
description.juvenile: Brownish-grey overall.
description.in_flight: Strong, direct flight in small flocks.

behaviour: Highly social, forms flocks; feeds largely on fruit in the canopy.
food: Fruits, berries, and insects.

habitat: Dense scrub and undergrowth in forests, especially along hillsides.

description.adult_male: Unstreaked rufous-brown cap and body, pale throat, and a relatively long tail.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, less rufous.

behaviour: Highly social, moves in small, noisy groups, foraging through thick bushes and low branches.
food: Insects and berries.

photos.caption: An endemic species...
photos.license: Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 4.0 International l

habitat: Still or slow-moving clean water bodies: streams, lakes, ponds, backwaters.

description.adult_male: Small, electric blue upperparts, turquoise back, rufous underparts, long black bill.
description.adult_female: Similar to male, but the lower mandible (jaw) is red/orange with a black tip.
description.juvenile: Duller, sooty breast band.
description.in_flight: Very fast, low flight over water, showing brilliant blue flash.

behaviour: Perches low and dives headfirst into the water to catch fish; solitary and highly territorial.
food: Small fish and aquatic insects.

habitat: Forest clearings, cultivated areas near water, open woodland.

description.adult_male: Brilliant green body, prominent chestnut head and breast, black throat band, no long tail streamers.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller green, chestnut head less pronounced.

behaviour: Feeds high up, catching flying insects from a prominent perch; highly social.
food: Bees, wasps, and other flying insects.

habitat: Forests and groves, specializing on the trunks and branches of trees.

description.adult_male: Sleek blue-grey upperparts, purple throat/breast, bold red bill, and a black forehead patch (velvet).
description.adult_female: Similar to male, but lacks the black facial stripe.
description.juvenile: Duller, paler colours.

behaviour: Unique among birds for climbing head-first down tree trunks; often part of mixed-species foraging flocks.
food: Insects and spiders gleaned from bark crevices.

habitat: Open forest, scrub, cultivated land, gardens.

description.adult_male: Black head/breast, rufous-brown body, long grey tail with black tip, grey wings with black primaries.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, brownish-grey head.
description.in_flight: Undulating, clumsy flight; very long tail conspicuous.

behaviour: Loud, unmistakable 'bob-o-link' call; omnivorous and inquisitive; often seen climbing trees.
food: Fruits, seeds, insects, small reptiles, eggs, and nestlings.

habitat: Dry scrub, thorn forest, and sparse woodland edges.

description.adult_male: Black head and upperparts, white belly, bright orange-red rump and wing patches.
description.adult_female: Grey upperparts, yellow rump, black mask.
description.in_flight: Flies in small, active flocks, showing brilliant colour flashes.

behaviour: Feeds high in the canopy in mixed flocks, moving constantly.
food: Insects and caterpillars.

habitat: Open wooded country, gardens, deciduous forests.

description.adult_male: Sleek, smoky grey drongo with a slightly forked tail; lacks the crest or 'rackets' of the Greater Racket-tailed Drongo.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.in_flight: Agile, acrobatic flyer.

behaviour: Perches conspicuously on wires or high branches, sallying out to catch insects; excellent mimic of other bird calls.
food: Insects and nectar.

habitat: Dense evergreen and moist deciduous forests, especially in coastal and hill tracts.

description.adult_male: Large, heavy-bodied pigeon; brilliant metallic green back and wings, pale grey head and underparts, deep chestnut undertail coverts.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller green, less metallic sheen.
description.in_flight: Heavy, powerful flight high above the canopy.

behaviour: Strictly arboreal, feeding in the canopy; gives a deep, resonant 'hoom' call.
food: Large fruits and berries, swallowed whole.

habitat: Dry scrub, thorn forest, and dense riparian vegetation.

description.adult_male: Long-tailed cuckoo; dark glossy green above, deep blue bare skin patch around the eye (face), prominent white tips to the tail feathers.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, less glossy.
description.in_flight: Heavy, gliding flight; often seen scrambling through branches.

behaviour: Forages methodically for insects in foliage; rarely flies long distances.
food: Large insects, caterpillars, and small vertebrates.

habitat: Open woodland, cultivation, human settlements, ruins, and old trees.

description.adult_male: Small, stocky owl; grey-brown with heavy white spotting on the crown and back. Pale facial disc and large yellow eyes.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, less distinct spotting.

behaviour: Crepuscular and often diurnal; perches conspicuously; known for its familiar 'chiree-chiree-chiree' calls.
food: Insects, small mammals, and small birds.

habitat: Grasslands, farmlands, damp fields, always near livestock.

description.adult_male: Stocky white egret. Breeding plumage: orange-buff plumes on the head, breast, and back; reddish bill/legs.
description.adult_female: Similar, but less intense buff plumes in breeding season.
description.juvenile: Entirely white with black bill.
description.in_flight: Flies with neck retracted.

behaviour: Follows grazing cattle and buffalo to catch insects disturbed by their movement.
food: Insects (especially grasshoppers), spiders, and small vertebrates.

habitat: Shallow wetlands, salt pans, and flooded fields.

description.adult_male: Pied plumage: black back/wings, white head/body. **Extremely long, thin pink legs.**
description.adult_female: Similar, but the black plumage is often browner.
description.juvenile: Brown-grey where the adult is black.
description.in_flight: Long pink legs trail far behind the body.

behaviour: Wades in shallow water, sweeping its bill to find aquatic prey; graceful and vocal.
food: Aquatic insects, small crustaceans, and mollusks.

habitat: Moist deciduous and evergreen forests, forest edges.

description.adult_male: Small, greenish-yellow warbler with a distinct double pale wing-bar, a prominent pale supercilium (eyebrow), and a faint crown stripe.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.

behaviour: Active, restless, constantly gleaning insects from leaves and twigs in the middle and upper canopy.
food: Small insects and larvae.

habitat: Large rivers, broad reservoirs, estuaries, coastal backwaters and marshy lakes; prefers extensive open water with abundant fish.

description.adult_male: Striking, medium-large raptor with dark brown upperparts contrasting with clean white underparts. Head white with a bold, broad dark eyestripe that extends to the nape. Wings long, narrow and angular with a kink at the carpal joint. Breast usually clear white or lightly marked.
description.adult_female: Similar to male but typically shows a darker, more heavily streaked 'breastband' across the upper chest; also slightly bulkier.
description.juvenile: Scalloped appearance on upperparts due to pale buff feather edges; head pattern similar to adults but tones warmer. Underparts often more buff-tinged.
description.in_flight: Wings held in a characteristic shallow 'M' shape with pushed-forward wrists; strong gliding interspersed with deep, elastic wingbeats. White underwing with dark carpal patches. Will frequently hover briefly before a dive.

behaviour: Specialised fish-hunter—dives feet-first from height, sometimes fully submerging. Can hover over water while locating prey. Often seen perched on snags or pylons near large waterbodies. Migratory individuals frequently stop over at reservoirs and estuaries in western India. Generally solitary.
food: Almost exclusively fish, captured by dramatic plunge-dives; uses reversible outer toes and spiny foot pads to secure slippery prey.

photos.caption: Osprey fishing near Bhigwan

habitat: Dense undergrowth, thickets, and forest floor.

description.adult_male: Brown upperparts, white throat, chestnut flanks, and a prominent, long, down-curved yellow bill (scimitar-like).
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, shorter bill.

behaviour: Feeds on the ground or low in bushes; known for its loud, repetitive 'whoop-whoop' duet calls.
food: Insects and grubs found in leaf litter.

habitat: Evergreen and moist deciduous forests, often high in the canopy.

description.adult_male: Glossy black head and back, brilliant orange-red underparts, rump, and wing patches.
description.adult_female: Yellow replaces the orange-red of the male; grey head/back.
description.in_flight: Flies in loose flocks, showing striking flashes of black and orange/yellow.

behaviour: Feeds actively in the canopy, often in small groups, giving persistent whistling calls.
food: Insects, spiders, and caterpillars.

habitat: Dense evergreen forests of the lower hills of the Western Ghats.

description.adult_male: Black head, chestnut mantle, clean white belly, and grey rump; long black and white tail.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.

behaviour: Arboreal and inquisitive; moves through the canopy alone or in small parties; loud, varied calls.
food: Insects, small reptiles, berries, and fruits.

habitat: Urban areas, cultivation, open country, near human habitation.

description.adult_male: Dark brown body, black hood, bright yellow bill, legs, and bare skin around the eye. Prominent white wing patches visible in flight.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, brownish hood.
description.in_flight: White wing patch is a key identifier.

behaviour: Bold, noisy, highly social; walks on the ground, often in pairs, feeding opportunistically.
food: Insects, seeds, fruit, food scraps.

habitat: Near water, large rivers, lakeshores, irrigation canals, village ponds.

description.adult_male: Large wagtail; black upperparts and breast, white belly. Distinctive white supercilium and long black-and-white tail.
description.adult_female: Black areas are sooty-grey.
description.juvenile: Duller, brownish-grey.

behaviour: Walks briskly on the ground, constantly wagging its tail up and down; feeds close to water.
food: Insects and aquatic invertebrates.

habitat: Dense undergrowth in subtropical and tropical moist forests, from plains to higher hills; forest edges and secondary growth.

description.adult_male: Dark brownish-grey upperparts, broad black mask through eye, white throat. Grey breast, rufous belly and underside. Uniformly colored tail darker than back. Heavy bill with pale yellow lower mandible.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller than adults, less distinct mask.
description.in_flight: Direct flight low through undergrowth, rarely flies in open.

behaviour: Extremely gregarious, moving in flocks of 6-40 birds. Skulks in undergrowth, feeding on ground by turning over leaves. Very vocal with loud shrill chattering, cackling and churring calls. Calls resemble those of Rufous Babbler in some instances.
food: Insects, fallen seeds, berries; forages mainly on ground among leaf litter.

habitat: Shola forests (isolated cloud forests at high altitudes) interspersed with montane grasslands; dense understory of moist evergreen forests above 1200m.

description.adult_male: Scaled pattern throughout—dark brown upperparts with buff spots forming scale-like appearance. Underparts buff-white with dark brown crescent-shaped markings creating scaled effect. Head and neck greyish with scaling.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: More heavily spotted than adults.
description.in_flight: Distinctive black band on white underwings visible in flight, similar to Siberian Thrush.

behaviour: Secretive and shy, keeping to dark understory and forest floor. Often seen on rainy days along forest roads. Forages on ground, turning over leaf litter. Scaled plumage provides excellent camouflage.
food: Opportunistic insectivore; feeds primarily on insects and other invertebrates from forest floor.

habitat: Dense montane evergreen forest (shola) and stunted rhododendron thickets at high elevations.

description.adult_male: Dark olive-brown thrush-like bird with a prominent black mask and white supercilium. Rich rufous patch on the belly/flanks.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, less distinct facial markings.

behaviour: Highly vocal, usually moving in noisy, close-knit groups; feeds on the ground and low bushes. This species has a very restricted distribution in high elevation areas of the Nilgiris and adjoining ranges
food: Insects, grubs, berries.

photos.caption: A Nilgiri Laughingthursh in Kothagiri, Tamilnadu, India

habitat: Evergreen and moist deciduous forests between 450-1600m elevation; also visits coffee, tea, rubber plantations and cultivated areas.

description.adult_male: Bluish-grey head, neck, breast and back; green lores and upper cheeks with bluish tinge; black band across chin with bright blue-green lower border forming collar. Blue wings and primaries. Long blue tail tipped yellow. Bright red upper mandible with yellow tip, brown lower mandible. Light yellow iris.
description.adult_female: Similar to male but smaller; breast and back greenish; lacks blue-green border below black collar; lores only faintly greenish; both mandibles blackish. Greyish-yellow underparts. Shorter tail.
description.juvenile: Greyish-green head; black collar faintly marked or absent; lacks green band below collar. Bill pale orange-red initially, turning black at 6 weeks; in males becomes red again after 9 months.
description.in_flight: Swift, straight flight through forest clearings. Long blue tail tipped yellow highly conspicuous. Dark blue wings contrast with grey body. Makes distinctive harsh screeching 'keek-keek-keek' calls while flying in flocks.

behaviour: Found in small groups of 4-5 birds or small flocks. Noisy and mostly noticed by harsh, raucous calls that differ from Plum-headed Parakeet. Almost always in tops of tall trees where difficult to detect. Only descends to ground in grain fields. Monogamous, pairing for life.
food: Seeds, fruits (especially figs, mangoes), small nuts, blossoms and nectar. Sometimes feeds on leaves of Loranthus longiflorus. May eat small insects during breeding season.

photos.caption: Male Malabar Parakeet near Mangaon, Raigad

habitat: Moist deciduous forests and sholas of Western Ghats; dense hill forests with fruiting trees.

description.adult_male: Large, dark pigeon. Blackish head, neck and underparts. Upperparts dark slaty-grey. Distinctive black and white checkerboard pattern on nape and sides of neck. Red orbital skin, red legs. Bill greyish with reddish base.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller than adults, less distinct checkerboard pattern on nape.
description.in_flight: Large size and dark appearance conspicuous. Heavy, direct flight through forest canopy.

behaviour: Mainly arboreal, foraging in canopy of dense hill forests. Usually seen singly, in pairs or small groups. Feeds on fruits in fruiting trees. Shy and wary, difficult to approach.
food: Frugivorous; feeds on variety of forest fruits and berries in canopy.

habitat: Dry deciduous scrub, thorny jungle, open woodland.

description.adult_male: Large woodpecker; buff/white underparts, golden-yellow back, and striking black throat/cheeks contrasting with a **white nape**. Male has a red crest.
description.adult_female: Lacks the red crest, which is black with white spots.
description.juvenile: Duller colours.

behaviour: Forages alone or in pairs, typically on stout trunks and thick branches; constantly drumming.
food: Insects and grubs under bark.

habitat: Large shallow lakes, flooded fields, marshes, river edges.

description.adult_male: Large white stork with a pinkish terminal band on the tertials (appearing pink in flight/resting). Bare yellow/orange head, deep pink bill with a down-curved tip.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Dull brown/grey, lacking the pink wash.
description.in_flight: Flies with neck outstretched, long legs trailing.

behaviour: Wades in shallow water, sweeping its half-open bill from side to side to locate prey by touch.
food: Fish, frogs, and large aquatic invertebrates.

habitat: Coastal mudflats, saline lakes, estuaries (often near the coast of the Sahyadris).

description.adult_male: Very large, pale pink/white bird with long, stilt-like legs, and a black-tipped bill bent sharply downwards.
description.adult_female: Similar, slightly smaller.
description.juvenile: Dull greyish-brown; legs duller pink.
description.in_flight: Wings show striking black primaries and pink coverts.

behaviour: Feeds with its head upside down, filtering algae and small organisms from the water.
food: Algae, diatoms, small crustaceans.

habitat: Open woodlands, cultivation with scattered trees, forest clearings.

description.adult_male: Sleek, smoky grey/ashy plumage, long pointed wings, and a short, stout blue bill with a black tip.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Brownish with streaked plumage.
description.in_flight: Glides frequently on wide, triangular wings; often seen in flocks.

behaviour: Highly aerial, catching insects in flight; noted for huddling together on branches in cold weather.
food: Flying insects.

habitat: Scrub jungle, dry grasslands, stony hillsides in the rain-shadow areas.

description.adult_male: Stocky lark, streaky brown upperparts, rufous patch on the wings (visible in flight), and a distinct pale supercilium.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller, less clearly marked.
description.in_flight: Aerial display flight is noisy and undulating.

behaviour: Terrestrial; often detected by its song and display flight over breeding territory.
food: Seeds and insects.

habitat: Dense forest undergrowth, particularly along streams and wet areas.

description.adult_male: Cryptic plumage (grey/brown/white mottling), huge broad head, and a wide frog-like mouth. Males are generally greyer.
description.adult_female: Reddish-brown morph also common.

behaviour: Strictly nocturnal; roosts motionless on low branches during the day, relying on camouflage.
food: Large nocturnal insects (moths, beetles).

photos.caption: Female on the right. Thattekad, Kerala.

habitat: Montane wet evergreen forests (shola) and dense hill scrub.

description.adult_male: Deep blue-black plumage, blue metallic spots on the head, chest, and wing-coverts. Often confused with the Malabar Whistling Thrush but smaller and with a different shape.
description.adult_female: Similar, slightly duller.

behaviour: Highly secretive, forages low in dense undergrowth. Known for its clear, human-like whistling song, often mistaken for the Thrush.
food: Insects and small invertebrates.

habitat: Tall, undisturbed evergreen forests, especially in high-density pockets of the Western Ghats.

description.adult_male: Massive bird; mostly black with white belly, white patches on wings/tail, and a huge, bright yellow and black casque/horn above the bill.
description.adult_female: Similar, but the casque is smaller and the black marking is more extensive.
description.juvenile: Small bill and casque.
description.in_flight: Slow, powerful wingbeats producing a noticeable whooshing sound.

behaviour: Arboreal and social; feeds primarily on fruit (especially figs); noisy, territorial call.
food: Fruits (figs are favoured), small mammals, reptiles, insects.

habitat: Open plains, cultivation, low hills, and dry deciduous forests.

description.adult_male: Brown upperparts, dark streaking below, distinctive white patch on the nape/throat, and prominent **white eyes** (iris).
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Browner eyes.
description.in_flight: Banded tail and dark patches near the wing tips visible when soaring.

behaviour: Perches conspicuously on telegraph poles or open branches, hunting insects and small vertebrates.
food: Lizards, small snakes, rodents, and large insects.

habitat: Large rivers, lakes, and estuaries with sandbanks for resting and nesting.

description.adult_male: Black crown and upperparts, white underparts, and an extremely large, **bright orange and yellow bill** with the lower mandible much longer than the upper.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Duller bill and streaked back.
description.in_flight: Flies low over the water, skimming the surface with the lower bill to catch fish.

behaviour: Highly social; feeds in groups, flying in long, straight lines just above the water surface.
food: Small surface-dwelling fish.

habitat: Open cultivated fields, grasslands, paddies, and short scrub.

description.adult_male: Streaky brown upperparts, buff-white breast with fine streaking, long tail, and thin legs.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Heavier streaking.
description.in_flight: Undulating flight, revealing white outer tail feathers.

behaviour: Terrestrial; walks briskly on the ground, often flicking its tail; known for its buzzing, repetitive song flight display.
food: Insects and small seeds.

habitat: Open scrub, dry stony areas, cultivated lands, often near water.

description.adult_male: Bright chestnut-red head and breast, yellow underparts, and olive-green back.
description.adult_female: Dull brownish-grey overall, yellow wash below, streaked back.
description.juvenile: Resembles female, duller.

behaviour: Highly gregarious in non-breeding season, feeding on the ground in large flocks, often near grain fields.
food: Seeds and grains.

habitat: Forest canopy, open wooded areas, gardens.

description.adult_male: Greenish-yellow warbler, distinguished by a yellowish **median crown stripe** and a distinct **double wing bar**.
description.adult_female: Similar to male.

behaviour: Highly active, constantly gleaning insects from the canopy and outer foliage.
food: Small insects and larvae.

habitat: Large shallow lakes, muddy marshes, tidal flats.

description.adult_male: Large white wading bird with long, black legs. Unmistakable long, flattened, spoon-shaped bill (yellow-tipped black).
description.adult_female: Similar to male.
description.juvenile: Black flight feathers.
description.in_flight: Flies with neck extended.

behaviour: Feeds by sweeping its bill side-to-side through shallow water to catch aquatic prey.
food: Aquatic insects, small fish, crustaceans.

photos.caption: Near Bhigwan

habitat: Open country, thin scrub, grasslands, often far from water.

description.adult_male: Bright emerald green plumage, blue tinge on the throat, black eye-line and a prominent black gorget (narrow collar). Long, slender central tail streamers.
description.adult_female: Similar to male, sometimes shorter tail streamers.
description.juvenile: Duller, lacks long tail streamers and gorget.
